shortDescription.text: Client-supplied MIME type used without server-side validation
Extension validation uses a blacklist instead of an allowlist, permitting unknown dangerous extensions
Uploaded file content (magic bytes) is never validated against declared type
TOOL_FILE transfer method unconditionally bypasses all file validation
Extension extracted via os.path.splitext only takes the last extension, allowing double-extension bypass

message.text: The client-supplied MIME type (file.mimetype from the multipart form) is passed directly to FileService.upload_file() and stored in the database without any server-side content-type detection. An attacker can upload a malicious executable with mimetype='image/png' to bypass any downstream MIME-based access controls or Content-Type headers served to other users.

File extension validation in FileService.upload_file() uses a configurable blacklist (UPLOAD_FILE_EXTENSION_BLACKLIST) rather than a strict allowlist. Any extension not explicitly blacklisted is permitted, meaning novel dangerous extensions (e.g., .hta, .cpl, .scr, .ps1, .jar, .elf) may be uploadable if the blacklist is incomplete. For non-'datasets' source uploads, there is no allowlist check at all beyond the blacklist.

No magic-byte or content-based file type validation is performed on uploaded files. The file content is stored as-is after only checking the extension and size. An attacker can upload a file with an innocuous extension (e.g., .png) but containing malicious content (e.g., an HTML file with embedded JavaScript, a polyglot PDF/JS file, or a web shell) which could be exploited if served or processed downstream.

In the workflow file validation logic, files with FileTransferMethod.TOOL_FILE bypass all validation checks unconditionally (allowed_file_types, allowed_file_extensions, transfer_methods). If an attacker can control or influence the transfer_method field to be TOOL_FILE, they can upload arbitrary file types that would otherwise be rejected by the configured FileUploadConfig. The FIXME comment in the code acknowledges this as a known issue.

Extension extraction uses os.path.splitext() which only returns the last extension. A filename like 'malware.php.png' yields extension 'png', passing the blacklist check while the original filename (stored in the database name field) retains the .php component. If any downstream system reconstructs the file path from the stored name rather than the UUID-based storage key, the double extension could lead to execution. Current storage is UUID-based which mitigates direct exploitation, but the stored filename could be used in download Content-Disposition headers.
